danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring evasion or combat; failure to eliminate before time limit results in level failure
Temporal constraint forcing player urgency; represents supercomputer simulation parameters

danger_signals.visual_form: Controlled character sprite becomes static captive state
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of active control; character must be rescued or permanently lost if next character dies

danger_signals.visual_form: Roster of available characters depletes visually
danger_signals.meaning: Approaching game-over state; remaining continues are final safety net

safety_signals.meaning: Safe destination; reaching with at least one character completes level objective

safety_signals.visual_form: Rescuable character sprites scattered throughout level
safety_signals.meaning: Resource expansion; can be escorted to exit to build party roster for subsequent levels

safety_signals.visual_form: Up to 20 distinct warrior sprites with unique visual designs
safety_signals.meaning: Strategic flexibility; different weapon types provide tactical options against varied enemy types

safety_signals.visual_form: Continue counter display (three total per game session)
safety_signals.meaning: Second-chance mechanism; allows tactical restart of failed levels with original three-character party

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Rescuable captive allies serve as visual indicators of allied presence requiring extraction

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Enemy sprite design varies by historical epoch theme (Ancient, Medieval, Modern, Future, Gain Ground System)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Enemy types reflect period-specific warfare aesthetics; thematic consistency reinforces simulation environment

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Entire game environment framed as digital simulation; level design reflects programmatic organization into four rounds of ten stages with themed historical epochs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies spawn and patrol according to simulation parameters; time limits reflect computational constraints; POW captives indicate simulated hostage scenario

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hostile simulation environment; enemies actively oppose player progression; thematic environmental design emphasizes hostile computational space
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All non-player entities function as threats; no neutral NPCs present; rescue mechanics create asymmetric relationship where allies are passive until escorted

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Historical epoch progression (Ancient → Medieval → Modern → Future → Gain Ground System)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level thematics change visually across rounds; environmental sprites, background designs, and architecture shift to reflect period aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy sprite design and behavior patterns adapt to epoch theme; level design complexity increases through progression; final epoch (Gain Ground System in Genesis version) represents meta-layer showing simulation internals

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Roster display differs; Normal shows three starting characters; Hard shows full twenty-character roster available immediately
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Normal Mode: captives appear throughout levels as resource collection; Hard Mode: captives removed, requiring strategic character deployment and permanent party composition

environmental_storytelling.example: Game progresses through Ancient, Medieval, Modern, Future epochs, each with visually distinct environments that communicate historical setting without explicit narrative exposition
Scattered POW captives throughout levels reinforce the supercomputer hostage scenario established in opening narrative; their distribution communicates danger and urgency
Final epoch (Gain Ground System in Genesis version) takes place within the simulation's core infrastructure itself, visually representing the computational nature of the conflict
Twenty recruitable warriors with distinct visual designs suggest varied military personnel from different backgrounds; reinforces setting of coordinated rescue operation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responds to player input; can move freely, attack enemies, interact with captives by walking over them
Transitions to static captive state; requires active character to escort to exit; disappears if subsequent characters fail level objective
Static position; visually distinct from active characters; awaiting player interaction
Follow escorting character to exit point; become active party members upon level completion
Patrol level; engage player characters on sight; must be eliminated or avoided to complete level objectives
Appear as level-10 encounters at end of each round; represent escalated threat requiring strategic character selection and tactical approach

notes: Gain Ground's world-state communication relies heavily on thematic environmental design across historical epochs and the framing device of a supercomputer simulation. The game uses visual character variety and captive mechanics to communicate narrative stakes (hostage rescue scenario). Danger signals are primarily temporal (countdown timer) and spatial (enemy presence). Safety is signaled through exit points and ally availability. The paradigm shift from Normal to Hard Mode is communicated through roster display changes. Limited documentation on specific visual design details; analysis based on gameplay mechanics described and narrative framing.
